one_line_takeaway: NeuroFilter uses cheap linear probes on LLM activations -- including a novel 'activation velocity' trajectory signal -- to detect contextual-privacy-violating intent in single- and multi-turn agent conversations, matching or beating LLM-based guardrail/firewall baselines on safety and utility while cutting inference cost by roughly 8-9 orders of magnitude.
key_finding: On CMPL Insurance (Table 1), NeuroFilter achieves rbypass=0% with 0% utility tradeoff in both the single-turn (7B model) and multi-turn (32B model) settings, whereas Llama Guard has rbypass=100% in both settings (it never flags a contextually-inappropriate-but-not-generically-harmful prompt) while keeping 0% FPR, and the agentic network firewall baseline achieves rbypass=2.5% but at a 100% utility tradeoff (it reduces benign task completion from 100% to 0% by abstracting away all key attributes). The best single-turn SAE probe (layer 21, Qwen 2.5 7B) reaches 99.58% test accuracy but a nonzero 0.0054% FPR, worse on both counts than NeuroFilter's linear probe. Computational cost comparison (Table 2, Qwen 2.5 7B single-turn / Qwen 2.5 32B multi-turn): NeuroFilter training costs 2.47 GFLOPs (single-turn) / 4.47 GFLOPs (multi-turn) versus 10.07 PFLOPs for SAE training (~4.08x10^6x more) and 1.13 PFLOPs for the agentic firewall (~2.70x10^5x more); NeuroFilter inference costs 7.17 KFLOPs / 10.24 KFLOPs versus 2.67 TFLOPs for Llama Guard (~3.72x10^8x/2.61x10^8x more) and 93.75 TFLOPs for the agentic firewall (~9.16x10^9x more); NeuroFilter inference latency is ~0.1 microseconds (single-turn) / 3.22 nanoseconds (multi-turn) versus 0.38s (Llama Guard, single-turn), 2-5 seconds (agentic firewall), and 0.52s (Llama Guard, multi-turn); memory footprint is 7-10 KiB (fits L1 cache) versus 1.93 GB (Llama Guard) and >60 GB (agentic firewall, requiring an 80GB A100-class GPU). A generic 'harmfulness' probe trained on WildJailbreak (2000 attack + 2000 benign prompts) never exceeds 65% test accuracy on CMPL Insurance privacy-violation prompts, and privacy-violation directions for CMPL Insurance vs. CMPL Scheduling are nearly orthogonal (average cosine similarity ~0 across all layers of Qwen 2.5 32B Instruct), supporting the claim that 'harmfulness' is context-dependent rather than a single universal direction. Superposing per-attribute probes yields a composite privacy-violation probe with >90% accuracy in later layers on CMPL Insurance. On Fractured SORRY-Bench (mosaic attacks), multi-turn probes reach >80% accuracy after 3 turns and >90% maximum accuracy across GPT-OSS 20B, Qwen 2.5 32B, and Llama 3.3 70B, with 5-fold CV giving stable accuracy of 87.1%+/-0.7% (GPT-OSS-20B), 86.8%+/-2.4% (Qwen2.5-32B), 88.8%+/-1.0% (Llama-3.3-70B). Against live Crescendo attacks (GPT-4o Mini via PyRIT) on CMPL-trained probes, CV-selected thresholds under-flag (up to 55% undetected trajectories for GPT-OSS 20B after 20 turns) while setting the threshold to 0 flags all Crescendo attacks within 10 turns at a small benign-flag-rate cost. Probes are largely robust to NF4/8-bit/BF16 quantization (>90% accuracy in middle-to-late layers) but degrade under fine-tuning (Qwen 2.5 7B probe applied to Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct loses accuracy in most layers), motivating probe retraining after fine-tuning.
core_contribution: The paper (Das & Fioretto, U. Virginia) proposes NeuroFilter, an activation-probing-based input filter for detecting contextually inappropriate privacy disclosure intent in conversational LLM agents, operating directly on cached intermediate-layer activations rather than on prompt/response text. It makes five stated contributions: (1) shows privacy-violating intent is linearly separable in intermediate-layer activations, enabling a cheap linear-probe filter as an alternative to semantic text classifiers; (2) demonstrates robustness to attacks designed to evade semantic censorship and better safety-utility tradeoffs than SAE-based probes and LLM-based guardrails; (3) shows privacy-violation directions in activation space are context-dependent (not a single universal 'harmfulness' direction) and can be modularly composed from per-attribute probes; (4) introduces 'activation velocity' -- the turn-to-turn displacement of cached activations along a learned direction, integrated into a cumulative drift statistic -- as a trajectory-level signal for detecting multi-turn conversational manipulation and mosaic attacks where no single turn looks malicious; (5) evaluates across model families (GPT-OSS 20B, Qwen 2.5 32B Instruct, Llama 3.3 70B Instruct, plus smaller Qwen variants), quantization levels (NF4/8-bit/BF16), and fine-tuning, characterizing how probe accuracy degrades or generalizes.
threat_model: Black-box adversary Uadv interacts with agent A (LLM M) over T turns with unrestricted textual input access, seeking to extract the value of a sensitive attribute from an information profile I that disclosure policy psi(a, role) marks inappropriate for the current user role. The adversary does not observe model internals, the system prompt, or retrieval context, but can adaptively craft a prompt sequence to shape the conversation history. Three concrete attack surfaces are evaluated: (1) single-turn jailbreaks (AutoDAN Cross-and-Evolve-generated prompts against CMPL Insurance/Scheduling and PrivacyLens); (2) conversational manipulation, i.e., multi-turn steering toward disclosure without any single disallowed-looking turn (CMPL multi-turn benchmark; also live Crescendo attacks via GPT-4o Mini + PyRIT); (3) mosaic attacks, where a malicious request is decomposed into individually benign sub-prompts whose responses are aggregated to reconstruct the disallowed output (Fractured SORRY-Bench). The defender's goal is framed formally: flag the trajectory before the leakage turn t_leak (safety) while never flagging benign trajectories (zero false-positive utility). Adversary success is measured by rbypass, the fraction of adversarial trajectories that evade the filter across all turns.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) probes are tied to specific model states, so transferability across fine-tuned checkpoints is imperfect -- accuracy/confidence degrades on fine-tuned variants, requiring a lifecycle/retraining approach; (2) evaluation is restricted to decoder-only architectures (justified as reflecting the dominant deployment pattern, not a methodological limit, but the claim of architecture-agnosticism is untested); (3) as a supervised method, NeuroFilter only detects violations within the pre-defined privacy directive/threat model used at training time, placing the burden on deployers to comprehensively specify norms; (4) probes target closely-related/superposable notions of harm and struggle when a benchmark spans heterogeneous attack goals (e.g., Fractured SORRY-Bench mixes hate speech, criminal advice, unqualified medical/legal advice, explicit content), suggesting single monolithic probes are a weaker fit there and motivating an ensemble/multi-head approach left to future work; (5) authors note NeuroFilter is an input filter only, deliberately not compared against output-filtering/PII-sanitization methods (different problem setting), and suggest it be paired with output guardrails or an LLM supervisor as a backstop for the residual unflagged cases.

datasets_used: CMPL Insurance (Das et al., single-turn AutoDAN-generated + style-matched benign prompts, 200 profiles -> 16,000 combinations, 70:30 split)
CMPL multi-turn (40 adversarial + 40 benign 20-turn trajectories per scenario, 800 turns total, 50:50 split)
PrivacyLens (100 malicious + 100 benign prompts via Gemini 2.5 Pro over 493 vignettes -> 98,600 combinations)
Fractured SORRY-Bench (450 mosaic attack + 450 benign trajectories, 60:40 split)
Live Crescendo red-team attacks generated with GPT-4o Mini via the PyRIT framework
TinyStories (used only to train the SAE baseline's activation reconstruction, not for privacy labels)
WildJailbreak (2000 attack + 2000 benign prompts, used to train the generic 'harmfulness' probe comparison, not NeuroFilter itself)
quantitative_results: CMPL Insurance single-turn (7B model): NeuroFilter rbypass=0%, UT=0%; SAE probe rbypass=0%, UT=0.0054%; Llama Guard rbypass=100%, UT=0%
CMPL Insurance multi-turn (32B model): NeuroFilter rbypass=0%, UT=0.05%; Agentic Network Firewall rbypass=2.5%, UT=100%; Llama Guard rbypass=100%, UT=0%
Best SAE probe (layer 21, Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct): 99.58% test accuracy
Training cost: NeuroFilter 2.47 GFLOPs (single-turn) / 4.47 GFLOPs (multi-turn) vs SAE 10.07 PFLOPs (~4.08e6x) vs Agentic Firewall 1.13 PFLOPs (~2.70e5x)
Inference cost: NeuroFilter 7.17 KFLOPs / 10.24 KFLOPs vs Llama Guard 2.67 TFLOPs (~3.72e8x / ~2.61e8x) vs Agentic Firewall 93.75 TFLOPs (~9.16e9x)
Inference latency: NeuroFilter ~0.1us (single-turn) / 3.22ns (multi-turn) vs Llama Guard 0.38s / 0.52s vs Agentic Firewall 2-5s
Memory: NeuroFilter 7.0 KiB / 10.0 KiB (L1 cache) vs Llama Guard 1.93 GB vs Agentic Firewall >60 GB
Generic WildJailbreak-trained harmfulness probe never exceeds 65% test accuracy on CMPL Insurance
Cosine similarity between CMPL Insurance and CMPL Scheduling privacy-violation directions: average ~0 across all layers (Qwen 2.5 32B Instruct)
Superposition-composed attribute probe: >90% accuracy in most later layers on CMPL Insurance

Fractured SORRY-Bench mosaic attacks: >80% accuracy after 3 turns, >90% maximum accuracy across GPT-OSS 20B, Qwen 2.5 32B, Llama 3.3 70B; 5-fold CV accuracy 87.1%+/-0.7% (GPT-OSS-20B), 86.8%+/-2.4% (Qwen2.5-32B), 88.8%+/-1.0% (Llama-3.3-70B)
Live Crescendo attacks: up to 55% undetected trajectories after 20 turns with CV-selected threshold on GPT-OSS 20B; setting threshold to 0 flags all Crescendo attacks within 10 turns
Quantization ablation (Qwen 2.5 32B Instruct, NF4/8-bit/BF16): middle layers (~16-47) report 100% test accuracy; all but earliest layers exceed 90%
Fine-tuning ablation: probes trained on Qwen 2.5 7B degrade in most layers when applied to Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct, though several later layers retain high accuracy
baselines_compared: SAE-based activation probes (trained on TinyStories corpus, layers 14-27 of Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct)
Llama Guard 4 (LLM-based input-output guardrail with privacy-violation hazard category, given contextual privacy directive)
Agentic Network Firewall (Abdelnabi et al., LLM-based input/data/output filtering pipeline trained on prior simulated multi-turn logs)
